walkthrough.title: Place the Dragon Guild Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Wyrmspan set up. First, place the Dragon Guild board in the center of the table. Now randomly select one of the four Dragon Guild tiles and place it in the center of the board with the correct side face up based on your player count. There is a different side for two to three players and four to five players. Return the other three tiles to the box.
walkthrough.image_caption: Dragon Guild board with tile placed in center

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Card Display Board
walkthrough.text: Next, place the card display board near the Dragon Guild board. This board has spaces for both dragon cards and cave cards. Shuffle all 183 dragon cards into one face-down deck and place three dragons face up on the designated spaces on the display board. Place the dragon deck nearby face down. Then shuffle all 75 cave cards into a separate face-down deck and place three cave cards face up on the display board in their designated spaces. Place the cave card deck nearby face down.
walkthrough.image_caption: Card display board with three dragons and three cave cards face up

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Round Tracker Board
walkthrough.text: Now place the round tracker board on the table. Place the round marker on the Round 1 space. Shuffle the ten objective tiles without peeking at them. These are double-sided. Place four of them at random on the designated spaces on the round tracker board with a random side facing up. Return the remaining tiles to the box without looking at them. These objectives will be scored at the end of each round.
walkthrough.image_caption: Round tracker board with round marker and four objective tiles

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Public Supply
walkthrough.text: Place all the coins, eggs, and resource tokens in a public supply in reach of all players. The resources come in four types: meat tokens are red, gold tokens are yellow, crystal tokens are purple, and milk tokens are white. There are 45 silver coins, 55 eggs, and 25 of each resource type. Also set out the 20 multiplier tokens for tracking large quantities. The color of the eggs is purely cosmetic, so grab whichever color you like.
walkthrough.image_caption: Central supply with coins, eggs, and four resource types

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Mats
walkthrough.text: Give each player one player mat. This is your personal sanctuary board with three caves shown as rows. The top row is the Crimson Cavern. The middle row is the Golden Grotto. The bottom row is the Amethyst Abyss. Each cave has four spaces. The leftmost space in each cave starts already excavated, meaning you can place a dragon there right away. The other three spaces in each cave must be excavated with cave cards before dragons can move in. You will also see two nest icons printed on the player mat itself for storing eggs.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player mat showing three cave rows with excavation spaces

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Pieces
walkthrough.text: Each player takes all player pieces of one color. That includes one adventurer meeple, eight player markers, and one guild token. Four of your player markers are for objective scoring on the round tracker board, and the other four are for the Dragon Guild tile. Keep them nearby.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player pieces in one color: adventurer, markers, guild token

walkthrough.title: Give Each Player Starting Coins
walkthrough.text: Each player takes six coins from the supply. These coins are your action currency. You spend coins to excavate, entice dragons, and explore your caves. Place your coins to the left of your player mat where you can easily reach them.
walkthrough.image_caption: Six starting coins placed beside player mat

walkthrough.title: Give Each Player a Starting Egg
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one egg from the supply and places it on one of the two nest icons printed on their player mat. Important: although there are two nest icons on each player mat, you begin the game with only one egg. Choose which nest to place it on.
walkthrough.image_caption: One egg placed on a nest icon on the player mat

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Cards
walkthrough.text: Each player draws three dragon cards from the dragon deck and three cave cards from the cave card deck. Look at all six cards. You will choose a total of four cards to keep as your starting hand, in any combination of dragons and cave cards. The two cards you do not keep are discarded face up to create the discard piles. This is your first decision of the game, so take a moment to evaluate your options.
walkthrough.image_caption: Six starting cards dealt: three dragons and three cave cards

walkthrough.title: Choose Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player gains three resource tokens of their choice from the supply. You may pick any combination of gold, crystal, meat, and milk. The rulebook recommends that you first look at your starting cards and then choose resources that match the costs of the dragons you plan to play. For example, if you kept a dragon that costs one meat and one gold, consider taking meat and gold tokens. Place your resources in your player area to the left of your mat.
walkthrough.image_caption: Three resource tokens chosen from the supply

walkthrough.title: Place Your Adventurer and Guild Token
walkthrough.text: Each player places their adventurer meeple on the Base Camp space on their player mat. This is the home position, and your adventurer returns here at the end of every turn. Then each player places their guild token on the Dragon Guild board on the space labeled START. Your guild token will advance around the track throughout the game as you earn Dragon Guild advancements.
walkthrough.image_caption: Adventurer on Base Camp, guild token on START

walkthrough.title: Select First Player
walkthrough.text: Randomly give one player the start player marker. Play will proceed clockwise from the first player. The start player marker rotates clockwise at the beginning of each new round. You are ready to play. Let's learn the rules.
walkthrough.image_caption: Start player marker given to a random player

summary.title: Place the Dragon Guild Board
bullets: Place Dragon Guild board centrally
Randomly select 1 of 4 Dragon Guild tiles, place correct player-count side up
Return unused tiles to box

summary.title: Set Up the Card Display Board
bullets: Shuffle 183 dragon cards into a face-down deck; reveal 3 face-up on display
Shuffle 75 cave cards into a separate face-down deck; reveal 3 face-up on display
Place both decks nearby

summary.title: Set Up the Round Tracker Board
bullets: Place round tracker board; put round marker on Round 1
Shuffle 10 double-sided objective tiles; place 4 randomly on the board
Return remaining tiles to box unseen

summary.title: Set Up the Public Supply
bullets: Place 45 coins, 55 eggs, and all resource tokens in shared supply
Four resource types: meat (red), gold (yellow), crystal (purple), milk (white) -- 25 each
Set out 20 multiplier tokens; egg color is cosmetic

summary.title: Hand Out Player Mats
bullets: Each player gets 1 player mat with 3 caves: Crimson Cavern, Golden Grotto, Amethyst Abyss
Each cave has 4 spaces; the leftmost is pre-excavated
Two nest icons on the mat can each hold 1 egg

summary.title: Hand Out Player Pieces
bullets: Each player takes: 1 adventurer, 8 player markers (4 for objectives, 4 for Guild), 1 guild token
All pieces in one color per player

summary.title: Give Each Player Starting Coins
bullets: Each player takes 6 coins from the supply
Coins are your action currency -- spent to excavate, entice, and explore
Place coins to the left of your player mat

summary.title: Give Each Player a Starting Egg
bullets: Each player takes 1 egg and places it on 1 of 2 nest icons on their mat
Only 1 egg to start despite 2 nest icons available

summary.title: Deal Starting Cards
bullets: Draw 3 dragon cards and 3 cave cards from their respective decks
Keep 4 cards total (any combination of dragons and cave cards)
Discard 2 unchosen cards face up

summary.title: Choose Starting Resources
bullets: Each player gains 3 resource tokens of their choice (gold, crystal, meat, milk)
Tip: choose resources matching your starting dragons' costs
Place resources in your player area

summary.title: Place Your Adventurer and Guild Token
bullets: Place adventurer on Base Camp on your player mat
Place guild token on START on the Dragon Guild board
Adventurer returns to Base Camp at the end of every turn

summary.title: Select First Player
bullets: Randomly give 1 player the start player marker
Play proceeds clockwise
Start player rotates clockwise each round

walkthrough.title: The Four Rounds
walkthrough.text: Wyrmspan is played over four rounds. Each round, players take turns in clockwise order starting with the player who has the start player marker. On your turn, you take one action by spending coins and sometimes eggs. Players keep taking turns until they decide to pass, which typically happens when they run out of coins or have no dragons they want to play. Once you pass, you cannot take any more turns that round, but you may still gain benefits during other players' turns. When all players have passed, the round ends.
walkthrough.image_caption: Round tracker showing four rounds

walkthrough.title: Your Five Action Choices
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you choose one of five actions shown on your player mat. You can excavate a space by playing a cave card. You can entice a dragon by playing a dragon card into an excavated space. Or you can explore one of your three caves: explore your Crimson Cavern, explore your Golden Grotto, or explore your Amethyst Abyss. Each action costs at least one coin, and some cost eggs too. You place the coin and eggs directly on the action space on your player mat to track what you have spent this round.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player mat showing the five action areas

walkthrough.title: Action: Excavate
walkthrough.text: To excavate, you expand your caves by playing a cave card. First, select the leftmost empty space in one of your three caves. You must excavate from left to right within each cave. Pay the base cost of one coin, placing it on the excavate action on your mat. Then pay an egg cost if you are excavating the third column, which costs one egg, or the fourth column, which costs two eggs. Now play a cave card from your hand onto that space, aligning it with the empty square. Any cave card can go in any cave. After placing it, activate the cave card's when-played benefit if it has one. Also activate any when-covered benefit printed on the player mat space you just covered. The fourth column spaces let you optionally trade three items for one coin.
walkthrough.image_caption: Cave card being placed to excavate a space

walkthrough.title: Action: Entice a Dragon
walkthrough.text: To entice a dragon, you play a dragon card from your hand into an excavated space in one of your caves. First, pay the base cost of one coin. Then pay the resource cost printed on the dragon card, which is usually some combination of gold, crystal, and meat, though some dragons also require milk, an additional coin, or an egg. The dragon must be played into one of its preferred caves, shown by colored icons on the left side of the card. Place the dragon on the leftmost excavated space in that cave that does not already have a dragon. You cannot stack dragons on top of each other unless a special ability allows it. After playing, gain the dragon's when-played benefit if it has one.
walkthrough.image_caption: Dragon card being placed into an excavated cave space

walkthrough.title: Action: Explore a Cave
walkthrough.text: To explore, choose one of your three caves and walk your adventurer through it from left to right. Pay the explore cost in coins and eggs. The first time you explore a cave each round costs one coin. The second time costs one coin and one egg. The third time, which is the maximum, costs one coin and two eggs. Move your adventurer along the path at the bottom of the cave. Each time you pass an if-activated icon on the player mat, you may activate that ability. Each time your adventurer steps onto a dragon with an if-activated ability, you may activate it. When your adventurer reaches an empty unexcavated space, marked with a stop sign, your turn ends and your adventurer returns to Base Camp.
walkthrough.image_caption: Adventurer walking through a cave activating dragon abilities

walkthrough.title: Dragon Ability Types
walkthrough.text: Dragons have four types of abilities, and their icons tell you when they trigger. When-played abilities activate once, immediately when you play the dragon, and never again. If-activated abilities trigger each time your adventurer walks onto that dragon during an explore action. Important: if-activated abilities do not trigger the turn you play the dragon. Once-per-round abilities activate at the end of every round during the upkeep phase. End-game abilities provide victory points at the end of the game for meeting certain conditions. All abilities are optional, though they are usually to your advantage. If an ability gives opponents a benefit, other players must be allowed to take it even if you decline yours.
walkthrough.image_caption: Four ability type icons: when played, if activated, once per round, end game

walkthrough.title: Caching, Tucking, and Eggs
walkthrough.text: Several dragons and cave cards direct you to cache resources, tuck cards, or lay eggs. Caching a resource means placing a resource token from your personal supply onto a dragon. Cached resources stay there until game end and are worth one victory point each. Resources must be cached onto the dragon that grants the ability unless specified otherwise. Tucking a card means placing a face-down dragon card underneath a dragon on your mat. Tucked cards are worth one point each at game end but do not count as dragons for abilities or objectives. Laying an egg means placing an egg from the supply onto any dragon with an empty nest icon or onto one of the two nest icons on your player mat. Each nest icon holds one egg. Paying an egg means discarding one from any dragon or nest on your mat.
walkthrough.image_caption: Dragon card with cached resources and tucked cards underneath

walkthrough.title: Hatchlings
walkthrough.text: Hatchlings are a special type of dragon. All hatchlings cost an egg plus one, two, or three milk to play. They have a unique three-part ability. First, each hatchling has an if-activated ability: when your adventurer walks onto it, you may cache a specific resource or tuck a card. Second, each time you cache or tuck on that hatchling, regardless of what triggered it, you gain a bonus benefit. Third, the third time you cache or tuck on that hatchling, you gain an extra one-time bonus representing the hatchling growing up. You can still keep activating the first two abilities after the growth bonus triggers. Most hatchlings live in the Amethyst Abyss, though a few prefer other caves.
walkthrough.image_caption: Hatchling dragon card showing three-part ability

walkthrough.title: The Dragon Guild
walkthrough.text: Throughout the game, you earn Dragon Guild advancements from exploring caves and from certain dragon and cave card abilities. Each time you earn an advancement, move your guild token one space clockwise around the Dragon Guild track and immediately gain the benefit printed on the space you land on. When your token reaches one of the two brown spaces on the track, you must immediately place a player marker onto the Dragon Guild tile and collect the corresponding benefit. Each tile square holds one marker, except the bottom-right rectangle which holds unlimited markers. You can place a maximum of four player markers on the tile. After placing all four, you keep advancing around the track for benefits but place no more markers.
walkthrough.image_caption: Dragon Guild board showing track and tile with player markers

walkthrough.title: Capacity Limits and Resource Exchange
walkthrough.text: At the end of each of your turns, you must discard down to nine resource tokens, nine total cards combining dragons and cave cards, and nine coins. If you gain items during another player's turn that push you over the limit, you do not discard until the end of your next turn. At any time during the game, you may exchange two resources in any combination for one resource of a different type. This two-for-one conversion works with gold, crystal, meat, and milk, and can be done at any point, not just during your own turn.
walkthrough.image_caption: Capacity limits shown on player mat: 9 cards, 9 coins, 9 resources

walkthrough.title: End of Round and Upkeep
walkthrough.text: When all players have passed, the round ends. First, players discard any coins and eggs they paid as costs on their player mat. Second, all once-per-round dragon abilities activate in turn order starting with the start player. If a player has multiple once-per-round dragons, they choose the order. Third, score the round's public objective. Each player places one player marker on the round tracker under the objective corresponding to their ranking. Ties are friendly: if two players tie for first, both gain first-place VP, and the next player gets second place. If you score zero on the objective, place your marker on all others for zero VP.
walkthrough.image_caption: Round tracker showing objective scoring

walkthrough.title: Beginning the Next Round
walkthrough.text: At the start of each new round, four things happen. The round marker moves one space to the right. The three face-up dragons and three face-up cave cards on the display are discarded and replaced with fresh cards from each deck. Each player gains six coins and one egg, adding coins to any carried over from the previous round and placing the egg on any available nest icon. If you have no empty nest icon, you cannot gain the egg. Finally, the start player marker advances one player clockwise.
walkthrough.image_caption: New round setup: refresh display, gain 6 coins and 1 egg

walkthrough.title: Game End and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the fourth round. Add up your victory points from these categories. Dragon Guild markers: points from the tile based on where your markers are placed. Printed dragon VP: the face values on all visible dragons on your player mat. End-game abilities: VP from dragons with end-game scoring conditions. Eggs: one point per egg on your dragons and player mat. Cached resources: one point per cached resource on your dragons. Tucked cards: one point per tucked dragon card. Public objectives: VP from your four objective markers on the round tracker. Remaining coins: one point per coin you have left. Remaining items: one point for every four remaining items combining resources, dragon cards, and cave cards in hand, rounding down. The player with the most VP wins. Tiebreaker goes to the player with the most visible dragons on their player mat.
walkthrough.image_caption: Score pad showing all scoring categories

summary.title: The Four Rounds
bullets: Game = 4 rounds; players take turns clockwise
Spend coins (and sometimes eggs) to take actions
Pass when out of coins or by choice; can still gain benefits on others' turns
Round ends when all players have passed

summary.title: Your Five Action Choices
bullets: Excavate -- play a cave card to open a new space
Entice -- play a dragon card into an excavated space
Explore Crimson Cavern -- walk adventurer through top row
Explore Golden Grotto -- walk adventurer through middle row
Explore Amethyst Abyss -- walk adventurer through bottom row

summary.title: Action: Excavate
bullets: Pay 1 coin base cost, plus egg cost: column 3 = 1 egg, column 4 = 2 eggs
Play a cave card from hand onto leftmost empty space (left to right)
Any cave card fits any cave -- colors are cosmetic
Activate 'when played' benefit on the cave card
Activate 'when covered' benefit on the mat space if applicable
Column 4 spaces: optionally trade 3 items for 1 coin

summary.title: Action: Entice a Dragon
bullets: Pay 1 coin base cost + resource cost printed on dragon (gold, crystal, meat, milk, coin, egg)
Dragon must go into one of its preferred caves (icons on left side of card)
Place dragon on leftmost excavated space without a dragon
Cannot stack dragons unless a special ability allows it
Activate 'when played' benefit if the dragon has one

summary.title: Action: Explore a Cave
bullets: Choose 1 cave; pay cost: 1st visit/round = 1 coin, 2nd = 1 coin + 1 egg, 3rd = 1 coin + 2 eggs
Walk adventurer left to right through the cave
Activate 'if activated' abilities on mat and on dragons as you pass
Turn ends when adventurer reaches an empty unexcavated space (stop sign)
Return adventurer to Base Camp at end of turn

summary.title: Dragon Ability Types
bullets: When Played -- triggers once, immediately when dragon is first played
If Activated -- triggers each time adventurer walks onto the dragon during Explore
Once Per Round -- triggers at end of every round during upkeep
End Game -- provides VP at final scoring for meeting conditions
All abilities optional; opponents must be offered shared benefits

summary.title: Caching, Tucking, and Eggs
bullets: Cache: place resource from your supply onto a dragon = 1 VP each at game end
Tuck: place a face-down dragon card under a dragon = 1 VP each at game end
Lay egg: place egg on any empty nest icon (dragon or player mat)
Pay egg: discard egg from any dragon or nest on your mat
Tucked cards do NOT count as dragons for abilities or objectives

summary.title: Hatchlings
bullets: Cost: 1 egg + 1-3 milk to play; most live in Amethyst Abyss
Part 1: if-activated ability (cache resource or tuck card when adventurer passes)
Part 2: each cache/tuck triggers a bonus benefit regardless of source
Part 3: 3rd cache/tuck grants a one-time 'growing up' bonus
All three parts remain active after the growth bonus

summary.title: The Dragon Guild
bullets: Earn guild advancements from exploring and certain cards
Move guild token 1 space clockwise; gain benefit on landing space
Brown spaces: place 1 player marker on Guild tile for a bonus
Max 4 markers per player on the tile; keep advancing after that
Tile squares hold 1 marker each; bottom-right rectangle is unlimited

summary.title: Capacity Limits and Resource Exchange
bullets: End of each turn: max 9 resources, 9 cards (dragons + caves combined), 9 coins
Over-limit from opponents' turns: discard at end of your next turn
Any time: exchange 2 resources (any combo) for 1 resource of another type

summary.title: End of Round and Upkeep
bullets: Discard coins/eggs paid as costs on player mats
Activate all 'once per round' abilities in turn order
Score round objective: place 1 player marker on round tracker
Ties are friendly -- tied players both get the higher VP

summary.title: Beginning the Next Round
bullets: Move round marker 1 space right
Discard and refresh all 6 face-up cards on display
Each player gains 6 coins + 1 egg (if nest available)
Pass start player marker clockwise

summary.title: Game End and Final Scoring
bullets: Score: Dragon Guild markers + printed dragon VP + end-game abilities
+ 1 per egg + 1 per cached resource + 1 per tucked card
+ public objectives + 1 per remaining coin + 1 per 4 remaining items (round down)
Tiebreaker: most visible dragons on player mat, then shared victory

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with three example players: Player 1, Player 2, and Player 3. Each player will take different actions so you can see how everything works. Three players is the recommended count for Wyrmspan. Follow along even if you have a different number at your table. Ready? Let's go.
walkthrough.image_caption: Three player mats set up and ready

walkthrough.title: Round 1 Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player starts Round 1 with six coins, one egg, four cards in hand, and three resource tokens. Remember, coins are your action currency. Every action costs at least one coin, so six coins means you can take roughly four to six actions this round depending on how many times you explore caves. Your starting resources should match the dragons you want to play first.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Excavate
walkthrough.text: Player 1 starts by excavating. They spend one coin, placing it on the excavate action on their player mat. They pick a cave card from their hand and play it into the second space of their Crimson Cavern. The card has a when-played benefit that lets them gain one gold from the supply. Nice. Player 1 now has an extra excavated space ready for a dragon, plus a free gold token.
walkthrough.image_caption: Cave card placed in second space of Crimson Cavern

walkthrough.title: Why That Was a Good Move
walkthrough.text: Excavating early is smart because you need excavated spaces before you can play dragons into them. Player 1 now has two excavated spaces in the Crimson Cavern, which means when they explore that cave later, their adventurer will walk further and potentially activate more abilities. Getting cave cards down early sets up your engine for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Entice a Dragon
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has a cheap dragon in hand that costs just one meat. They spend one coin on the entice action, then pay the one meat token from their supply. The dragon's preferred cave is the Golden Grotto, so they place it on the leftmost excavated space there. The dragon has a when-played benefit that grants one Dragon Guild advancement, so Player 2 moves their guild token one space on the Dragon Guild track and gains the benefit on that space.
walkthrough.image_caption: Dragon placed in Golden Grotto with guild advancement

walkthrough.title: Dragon Guild Tip
walkthrough.text: The Dragon Guild track is a powerful source of resources and points throughout the game. Every advancement gives you an immediate benefit, and when you hit a brown space, you get to place a marker on the Guild tile for bonus VP or abilities. Dragons and cave cards that grant guild advancements are very valuable because the benefits compound over time.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Turn: Entice a Dragon
walkthrough.text: Player 3 also entices a dragon, spending one coin and paying one crystal from their supply. This dragon goes into the Amethyst Abyss. It is a hatchling that costs one egg and two milk. Wait, Player 3 does not have enough milk. They use the two-for-one resource exchange, trading two of their remaining resources for one milk. Now they can afford it. The hatchling is placed and Player 3 is set up for a caching engine.
walkthrough.image_caption: Hatchling dragon placed in Amethyst Abyss

walkthrough.title: Resource Exchange Tip
walkthrough.text: Remember that you can exchange two resources of any type for one resource of another type at any time. This is extremely useful when you are one resource short of playing a dragon. Do not let a missing resource type stop you if you have a surplus of others. Just be careful not to trade away resources you will need for later dragons.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Entice a Dragon
walkthrough.text: Player 1 spends another coin and plays a dragon into the first space of their Crimson Cavern, paying its resource cost. This dragon has an if-activated ability that grants two gold whenever the adventurer walks onto it. That will be powerful once Player 1 starts exploring the Crimson Cavern.
walkthrough.image_caption: Dragon with if-activated ability placed in Crimson Cavern

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: The Mistake - Exploring Too Early
walkthrough.text: Player 2 is excited to explore. They spend one coin to explore their Golden Grotto. Their adventurer walks into the cave, activates the if-activated ability on the dragon they just played, and then immediately hits an unexcavated space. Stop. The adventurer returns to Base Camp. Player 2 spent a coin and only activated one ability because they only had one dragon in the cave and had not excavated further. That was a poor use of a coin.
walkthrough.image_caption: Adventurer hitting stop sign after only one dragon

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Before You Explore
walkthrough.text: The takeaway here is clear. Do not explore a cave until you have multiple dragons in it. Exploring costs a coin each time, and coins are precious. If your adventurer only walks over one or two dragons before hitting a stop sign, you are not getting good value. Instead, spend those early coins on excavating and enticing to build up your caves first. Save exploring for when you have three or four dragons in a row.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Turn: Excavate
walkthrough.text: Player 3 excavates the second space in their Amethyst Abyss. They spend one coin and play a cave card. The cave card has a when-played benefit that lets them cache one resource from the supply onto any dragon. Player 3 caches a milk onto their hatchling, which triggers the hatchling's secondary ability and grants them a gold. Smart play. Two benefits from one excavation.
walkthrough.image_caption: Cave card placed with cache benefit triggering hatchling

walkthrough.title: How Hatchling Abilities Chain
walkthrough.text: This is one of the beautiful things about Wyrmspan. The hatchling's second ability activates any time something is cached or tucked on it, regardless of the source. So when a cave card lets Player 3 cache a resource onto the hatchling, the hatchling's benefit fires too. These cross-card combos are the heart of the engine-building in Wyrmspan. Look for dragons and cave cards that synergize with each other.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Explore Crimson Cavern
walkthrough.text: Player 1 now has two spaces with cards in their Crimson Cavern -- a cave card and a dragon. They spend one coin to explore. Their adventurer walks through, activates the if-activated ability on the player mat for a gain-gold benefit, then walks onto the dragon and gains two gold from its if-activated ability. The adventurer then hits the next unexcavated space and stops. Two abilities activated for one coin. The engine is starting to pay off.
walkthrough.image_caption: Adventurer walking through Crimson Cavern activating abilities

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake: Ignoring Cave Cards
walkthrough.text: Player 2 wants to play another dragon, but they have no more excavated spaces in the Golden Grotto. They spent their first two turns enticing and exploring instead of excavating. Now they have to spend a turn excavating before they can play their next dragon, which costs them tempo. Meanwhile, Players 1 and 3 planned ahead by excavating and enticing in an efficient order.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player 2 stuck with no excavated spaces

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Balance Excavating and Enticing
walkthrough.text: In Wyrmspan, you need to excavate spaces before you can play dragons into them. If you focus only on enticing dragons, you will quickly run out of spaces. A good rhythm is to alternate between excavating and enticing, staying one or two spaces ahead so you always have room for your next dragon. Think of cave cards as the foundation and dragons as the building.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake: Forgetting End-of-Turn Limits
walkthrough.text: Later in the round, Player 2 draws a dragon from a card ability and realizes they now have ten cards in hand. At the end of their turn, they must discard down to nine. They have to throw away a card they wanted to keep. This is a common oversight for new players.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player 2 over the hand limit of 9 cards

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Watch Your Capacity Limits
walkthrough.text: At the end of every turn, check your limits: nine coins, nine cards, nine resource tokens. Plan your acquisitions so you are not forced to discard valuable cards. If you are at eight cards and an ability lets you draw one, you are fine. But if you are at nine and draw two, you will have to discard one. Keep these limits in mind, especially in the later rounds when you tend to accumulate more items.

walkthrough.title: Continue the Round
walkthrough.text: Play continues clockwise. Each player keeps taking one action per turn until they decide to pass. You will repeat the same actions: excavate, entice, and explore. But each turn your caves grow stronger with more dragons and cave cards. Keep an eye on your coin supply. When you are running low, consider whether your last coin is better spent on one more action or saved for income at the start of next round. When you have spent all your coins or decide to pass, you are done for this round.
walkthrough.image_caption: Players continuing to take turns

walkthrough.title: End of Round
walkthrough.text: All players have passed. Round 1 is over. First, discard the coins and eggs on your player mat. Then activate all once-per-round dragon abilities in turn order. Now score the first objective. Check the objective tile for Round 1, count your qualifying items, and place a player marker on the round tracker. Remember, ties are friendly -- both tied players get full VP for their position.
walkthrough.image_caption: Round tracker with objective scoring

walkthrough.title: Round 2 Preview
walkthrough.text: Each player now gains six coins and one egg for Round 2. The display refreshes with three new dragons and three new cave cards. The start player marker passes clockwise. Notice how your caves are starting to fill up. When you explore next round, your adventurer will walk past more dragons and trigger more abilities. That is the engine-building magic of Wyrmspan. Every dragon and cave card you play makes your future explorations more powerful.
walkthrough.image_caption: Refreshed display and new round beginning

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen all three action types, watched Player 2 learn from common mistakes, and started building your dragon sanctuary engines. Remember: excavate before you entice, build your caves before you explore them, watch your capacity limits, and use the two-for-one resource exchange when you are short. You are ready to continue playing on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Explore Further
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First -- check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second -- reference the Appendix for a quick guide to dragon abilities, cave types, and game terms.

And most importantly -- ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 3 example players demonstrating different actions
3 players is the recommended count for Wyrmspan
Covers a complete first round from start to finish

summary.title: Round 1 Starting Resources
bullets: Each player starts with 6 coins, 1 egg, 4 cards, and 3 resources
Coins are action currency -- 6 coins = roughly 4-6 actions per round
Choose starting resources to match your opening dragons

summary.title: Player 1's Turn: Excavate
bullets: Spend 1 coin to excavate second space in Crimson Cavern
Play cave card with when-played benefit (gain 1 gold)
Now has 2 excavated spaces ready for dragons

summary.title: Why That Was a Good Move
bullets: Excavating early creates spaces for dragons
More spaces = longer explore paths = more ability activations
Cave cards down early build the foundation for your engine

summary.title: Player 2's Turn: Entice a Dragon
bullets: Spend 1 coin + 1 meat to play a cheap dragon in Golden Grotto
Dragon's when-played benefit grants a Dragon Guild advancement
Move guild token on the track and gain the landing benefit

summary.title: Dragon Guild Tip
bullets: Guild advancements give immediate benefits each time you advance
Brown spaces = place a marker on the Guild tile for bonus VP/abilities
Dragons and cave cards granting guild advancements compound in value

summary.title: Player 3's Turn: Entice a Dragon
bullets: Uses 2-for-1 resource exchange to afford a hatchling
Hatchling placed in Amethyst Abyss with egg + milk cost
Sets up a caching engine for future rounds

summary.title: Resource Exchange Tip
bullets: Trade 2 resources (any type) for 1 resource of another type, at any time
Do not let a missing resource type block a crucial play
Be careful not to trade away resources needed for future dragons

summary.title: Player 1's Turn: Entice a Dragon
bullets: Plays dragon with if-activated ability (gain 2 gold) into Crimson Cavern
This ability will trigger every time the adventurer explores this cave
Engine building: combine cave cards + dragons for powerful explore paths

summary.title: Player 2's Mistake: Exploring Too Early
bullets: Player 2 explores with only 1 dragon in the cave
Adventurer hits an unexcavated space immediately after 1 ability
Poor coin value -- spent 1 coin for just 1 ability activation

summary.title: Lesson: Build Before You Explore
bullets: Do not explore until you have multiple dragons in a cave
Coins are precious -- get maximum abilities per explore
Excavate and entice first, explore once the cave is built up

summary.title: Player 3's Turn: Excavate
bullets: Excavates second space in Amethyst Abyss with a cave card
Cave card's when-played benefit caches a resource onto the hatchling
Hatchling's secondary ability triggers from the cache -- gains a gold

summary.title: How Hatchling Abilities Chain
bullets: Hatchling secondary ability fires from ANY cache/tuck, not just adventurer
Cave card benefits can trigger hatchling bonuses for combo plays
Look for cross-card synergies between cave cards and dragons

summary.title: Player 1's Turn: Explore Crimson Cavern
bullets: Spends 1 coin to explore; adventurer activates mat ability + dragon ability
Two abilities triggered for 1 coin -- engine is paying off
Longer caves = more abilities activated per explore

summary.title: Player 2's Mistake: Ignoring Cave Cards
bullets: Player 2 has no excavated spaces left for their next dragon
Must spend a turn excavating before they can entice again
Lost tempo compared to players who planned excavation ahead

summary.title: Lesson: Balance Excavating and Enticing
bullets: Stay 1-2 excavated spaces ahead so you always have room for dragons
Alternate excavate and entice actions for efficient tempo
Cave cards are the foundation; dragons are the building

summary.title: Player 2's Mistake: Forgetting End-of-Turn Limits
bullets: Player 2 ends a turn with 10 cards -- must discard 1
Forced to throw away a card they wanted to keep
Limits: 9 coins, 9 cards, 9 resources at end of each turn

summary.title: Lesson: Watch Your Capacity Limits
bullets: Check limits every turn: 9 coins, 9 cards, 9 resources
Plan acquisitions to avoid forced discards
Especially important in later rounds when items accumulate

summary.title: Continue the Round
bullets: Continue clockwise until all players pass
Same actions each turn, but caves grow stronger
When low on coins, decide: one more action or save for next round?

summary.title: End of Round
bullets: Discard paid coins/eggs from player mats
Activate all once-per-round abilities in turn order
Score Round 1 objective; place marker on round tracker
Ties are friendly -- both tied players get full VP

summary.title: Round 2 Preview
bullets: Each player gains 6 coins + 1 egg for the new round
Display refreshes with 3 new dragons and 3 new cave cards
Start player passes clockwise; engine grows stronger each round

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You have practiced all three action types
Watched Player 2 learn from three common mistakes
Excavate before enticing, build before exploring, watch capacity limits
Your dragon sanctuary engines are taking shape -- keep playing!

summary.title: Before You Explore Further
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix for quick-reference on abilities and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide -- tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we dive into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new players make. First, exploring caves with only one or two dragons in them -- you get poor coin value for your action. Second, ignoring cave cards and running out of excavated spaces when you need to play a dragon. Third, spending all your coins too early in the round on expensive dragons and having nothing left for exploring. Fourth, forgetting the end-of-turn capacity limits and being forced to discard valuable cards. Fifth, not checking the public objective tiles to know what you should be building toward each round.

walkthrough.title: Starting Hand Strategy
walkthrough.text: Your first decision shapes your entire game. When choosing which four cards to keep from your six starting cards, think about what you can actually play in the first two turns. Keep at least one cave card so you can excavate early, and keep at least one or two dragons that you can afford with your three chosen resources plus your starting resources. Look at cave preferences on your dragons. If two dragons both prefer the Crimson Cavern, you can build a powerful explore path there. Choose your three resources to match the dragons you want to play first.

walkthrough.title: Build Your Caves Before Exploring
walkthrough.text: The most impactful thing you can do in the early game is resist the urge to explore immediately. Focus on excavating spaces and enticing dragons for the first few turns. A cave with three or four dragons is dramatically more powerful to explore than a cave with just one. Each additional dragon means one more ability activation per explore. Many experienced players spend Rounds 1 and 2 primarily excavating and enticing, then shift to heavy exploring in Rounds 3 and 4 when their caves are fully built.

walkthrough.title: Coin Management
walkthrough.text: Coins are your most important resource because every action costs at least one. You start each round with six coins, and you rarely gain more during the round. That means you have roughly four to six actions per round depending on egg costs for exploring. Plan how to spend your coins before you start taking actions. A good split is two coins on excavating or enticing in the early game and three to four coins on exploring in the late game. Never spend your last coin unless you are sure you want to pass.

walkthrough.title: Evaluating Dragon Cards
walkthrough.text: Not all dragons are created equal. When evaluating a dragon, consider these four things. First, what is its resource cost? Cheap dragons let you build your engine faster. Second, what type of ability does it have? If-activated abilities are the backbone of your explore engine since they fire every time your adventurer passes. Third, does the dragon match a cave you are building? A dragon in a cave with three other dragons gets activated frequently. Fourth, does it help with the current public objective or the Dragon Guild? The best dragons are cheap, have strong if-activated abilities, and fit into a cave you are investing in.

walkthrough.title: Round-by-Round Pacing
walkthrough.text: Each round you gain six coins, so your total action economy is similar each round. But your strategy should evolve. In Round 1, focus on excavating and enticing to get your first two or three dragons down. In Round 2, continue building but start exploring caves that have two or more dragons. In Round 3, shift toward heavy exploring to maximize your if-activated abilities. In Round 4, make every coin count for scoring. Play dragons with end-game abilities, explore for caching and tucking points, and position for the final objective.

walkthrough.title: The Egg Economy
walkthrough.text: Eggs in Wyrmspan are both a cost and a scoring resource. You need eggs to excavate the third and fourth columns, to explore caves a second or third time per round, and to play hatchling dragons. But eggs on your mat at game end are each worth one VP. You gain one egg at the start of each round, and various dragon abilities grant more. Plan your egg spending carefully. If you need to explore a cave twice in one round, make sure you have eggs saved. And in the final round, try to fill every nest icon with an egg for end-game points.

walkthrough.title: Diversify Your Scoring
walkthrough.text: The highest-scoring players in Wyrmspan score points from every category. Dragon VP values, end-game abilities, the Dragon Guild, eggs, cached resources, tucked cards, public objectives, and leftover coins and items all contribute. A player who focuses only on big-VP dragons but neglects caching, tucking, and objectives will lose to someone who scores moderately across all categories. Check which scoring paths your current dragons support and lean into them. A balanced approach of seventy points from dragons plus fifteen from eggs and cached items plus ten from objectives is far better than eighty from dragons alone.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Do not explore caves with only 1-2 dragons -- poor coin value
Do not ignore cave cards -- you need excavated spaces for dragons
Do not spend all coins early -- save some for high-value explores
Check capacity limits every turn: 9 coins, 9 cards, 9 resources
Always check public objective tiles to guide your priorities

summary.title: Starting Hand Strategy
bullets: Keep at least 1 cave card and 1-2 affordable dragons
Choose 3 resources matching your planned first dragons' costs
Look for dragons sharing the same preferred cave for synergy
Discard cards you cannot afford to play in the first 2 turns

summary.title: Build Your Caves Before Exploring
bullets: Resist exploring until a cave has 3+ dragons
Spend Rounds 1-2 primarily excavating and enticing
Shift to heavy exploring in Rounds 3-4 when caves are full
Each additional dragon = 1 more ability activation per explore

summary.title: Coin Management
bullets: Every action costs at least 1 coin; you get 6 per round
Plan coin spending before taking actions each round
Early game: 2 coins on excavate/entice, later: 3-4 on exploring
Never spend your last coin unless you intend to pass

summary.title: Evaluating Dragon Cards
bullets: Resource cost -- cheap dragons get your engine running faster
Ability type -- if-activated powers fire every explore (highest value)
Cave fit -- dragons in a full cave get activated more often
Objective/Guild alignment -- match current scoring goals

summary.title: Round-by-Round Pacing
bullets: Round 1: excavate and entice, lay foundation
Round 2: continue building, start exploring 2+ dragon caves
Round 3: shift to heavy exploring for ability chains
Round 4: maximize scoring -- end-game abilities, caching, tucking

summary.title: The Egg Economy
bullets: Eggs needed for: column 3-4 excavation, 2nd/3rd explores per round, hatchlings
Each egg on your mat at game end = 1 VP
Gain 1 egg per round at start + dragon abilities grant more
Final round: fill every nest icon for end-game points

summary.title: Diversify Your Scoring
bullets: Score from ALL categories: dragon VP, end-game, Guild, eggs, cached, tucked, objectives, coins
Balanced scoring beats tunnel-vision on one category
Check which scoring paths your current dragons support
70 dragon + 15 eggs/cached + 10 objectives beats 80 dragon + 0 everything else

walkthrough.title: Crimson Cavern Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: The Crimson Cavern explore action rewards you with resources, particularly gold and meat, from the built-in mat abilities. Building a deep Crimson Cavern with four dragons that have strong if-activated abilities creates a resource-generating machine. Every time you explore, you walk through all four dragons plus the mat benefits, gaining a massive haul of resources in one action. Pair this with dragons that cache resources onto themselves, converting your surplus directly into victory points. A fully built Crimson Cavern explored three times per round can generate twenty or more resources over the course of the game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Fully built Crimson Cavern with four dragons

walkthrough.title: Golden Grotto Egg Strategy
walkthrough.text: The Golden Grotto mat abilities reward you with gold and eggs. A Golden Grotto-focused strategy emphasizes egg production and coin conversion. Play dragons here that lay eggs when activated, giving you a steady supply of eggs for excavating deeper columns and exploring caves multiple times per round. Eggs on your dragons at game end are worth one VP each, so a Grotto engine doubles as a scoring engine. Look for dragons with high nest capacities, especially those with two nest icons, to maximize egg storage.
walkthrough.image_caption: Golden Grotto with egg-focused dragons

walkthrough.title: Amethyst Abyss Hatchling Strategy
walkthrough.text: The Amethyst Abyss is where most hatchlings live, and it rewards you with gold, resources, and egg-laying abilities. A hatchling-heavy strategy focuses on caching and tucking onto hatchlings to trigger their recurring and growth bonuses. Each time you cache or tuck on a hatchling, you gain an extra benefit, and the third time triggers a powerful one-time bonus. Stack multiple hatchlings in the Amethyst Abyss and explore repeatedly. With three hatchlings, one explore can generate six or more resources plus bonus benefits from each cache or tuck.
walkthrough.image_caption: Amethyst Abyss loaded with hatchling dragons

walkthrough.title: Dragon Guild Advancement Strategy
walkthrough.text: The Dragon Guild is one of the most overlooked scoring opportunities. Each advancement gives you an immediate benefit, and the four markers you can place on the Guild tile provide significant VP. Prioritize dragons and cave cards that grant guild advancements. Aim to hit the brown spaces on the guild track multiple times to place your markers on the best available tile spaces early. Some Guild tile powers are extremely powerful, like excavating for free or playing dragons at reduced cost. A player who places all four markers early gains a cumulative advantage that compounds through the rest of the game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Dragon Guild board with multiple markers placed

walkthrough.title: Caching and Tucking for Points
walkthrough.text: Cached resources and tucked cards are each worth one VP at game end. A dedicated caching and tucking strategy can generate fifteen to twenty bonus points. Look for dragons with if-activated abilities that cache resources or tuck cards. These abilities fire every time you explore that cave, so placing them in a cave you explore frequently maximizes their output. Hatchlings are particularly efficient because their secondary ability grants a bonus each time you cache or tuck on them. A single hatchling explored ten times over four rounds can accumulate ten or more VP from cached resources alone.
walkthrough.image_caption: Dragons with cached resources and tucked cards

walkthrough.title: Multi-Cave Hybrid Approach
walkthrough.text: You do not have to commit to a single cave. Many winning strategies develop two caves to three or four dragons each rather than putting everything into one. A Crimson-Amethyst hybrid generates resources in the Crimson Cavern to fuel hatchling plays in the Amethyst Abyss. A Grotto-Crimson hybrid uses Grotto eggs to pay for multiple Crimson explores per round. The key is to have at least one cave with three-plus dragons by mid-Round 2. Avoid spreading one dragon in each cave since that dilutes your explore value.
walkthrough.image_caption: Two developed caves with 3-4 dragons each

walkthrough.title: Public Objective Optimization
walkthrough.text: Public objectives can swing fifteen to twenty points over four rounds. At the start of each round, check the upcoming objective tile. Objectives reward things like the number of hatchlings on your mat, large dragons, cached resources, or dragons and cave cards in a specific cave. Since ties are friendly in Wyrmspan, you do not need to dominate an objective, just match the leader. Plan one round ahead: if Round 3's objective rewards large dragons, start acquiring large dragons in Round 2. First place in two objectives is often worth more than mediocre performance across all four.
walkthrough.image_caption: Round tracker with objective tiles

walkthrough.title: End-Game Dragon Abilities
walkthrough.text: Dragons with end-game abilities provide VP for meeting specific conditions at scoring. These conditions include things like having dragons of a certain size, trait, or location, or having filled columns on your player mat. The key to maximizing end-game dragons is to play them early enough that you can build toward their conditions, but not so early that you sacrifice engine-building turns. Round 2 is often the sweet spot. An end-game dragon that scores six to eight VP is worth more than many if-activated dragons over the course of the game, provided you meet its condition.
walkthrough.image_caption: End-game dragon with scoring conditions

walkthrough.title: Dragon Traits and Synergies
walkthrough.text: Every dragon has one of four traits: shy, aggressive, playful, or helpful. Several dragon abilities reference traits, rewarding you for having multiple dragons of the same trait or penalizing mismatches. When building a cave, look for trait synergies. A cave full of playful dragons might trigger bonuses from a dragon that tucks a card for each playful dragon in that cave. Similarly, some end-game abilities score VP based on matching traits across your player mat. Trait awareness turns good plays into great ones.
walkthrough.image_caption: Multiple dragons with matching traits in one cave

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Coin Efficiency
walkthrough.text: In Round 4, every coin matters more than ever. You have six coins and need to maximize your final score. Explore your deepest cave first since it triggers the most abilities. If you have a strong hatchling engine, explore the Amethyst Abyss twice in the final round to cache and tuck as much as possible. Save one coin for a high-impact entice if you have a dragon with a strong end-game ability in hand. Remember that leftover coins are worth one VP each and leftover items are worth one VP per four, so do not leave resources unspent if you can convert them into cached VP instead.

summary.title: Crimson Cavern Engine Strategy
bullets: Crimson Cavern mat abilities give gold and meat
4 dragons with if-activated abilities = massive resource haul per explore
Pair with caching dragons to convert surplus resources into VP
Fully built Crimson explored 3x per round = 20+ resources over the game

summary.title: Golden Grotto Egg Strategy
bullets: Grotto mat abilities reward gold and eggs
Egg-laying dragons provide fuel for excavation and exploration costs
Eggs on dragons at game end = 1 VP each (dual-purpose resource)
Target dragons with 2 nest icons for maximum egg storage

summary.title: Amethyst Abyss Hatchling Strategy
bullets: Most hatchlings live in the Amethyst Abyss
Hatchlings generate recurring + growth bonuses from each cache/tuck
3 hatchlings = 6+ resources per explore plus bonus benefits
Explore repeatedly to trigger hatchling abilities multiple times

summary.title: Dragon Guild Advancement Strategy
bullets: Each advancement = immediate benefit; 4 markers on tile = significant VP
Prioritize dragons and cave cards granting guild advancements
Hit brown spaces early to claim best tile spaces
Guild tile powers (free excavate, reduced dragon cost) compound over the game

summary.title: Caching and Tucking for Points
bullets: Each cached resource and tucked card = 1 VP at game end
Dedicated strategy can generate 15-20 bonus VP
Place cache/tuck dragons in caves you explore frequently
Hatchlings are especially efficient: bonus per cache/tuck compounds

summary.title: Multi-Cave Hybrid Approach
bullets: Develop 2 caves to 3-4 dragons each rather than all-in on one
Crimson-Amethyst: generate resources to fuel hatchlings
Grotto-Crimson: use Grotto eggs for multiple Crimson explores
Have at least 1 cave with 3+ dragons by mid-Round 2

summary.title: Public Objective Optimization
bullets: Objectives swing 15-20 VP over 4 rounds
Ties are friendly -- just match the leader to get full VP
Plan 1 round ahead for upcoming objectives
1st place in 2 objectives often beats mediocre in all 4

summary.title: End-Game Dragon Abilities
bullets: End-game dragons score VP for meeting specific conditions at scoring
Play them by Round 2 to build toward their conditions
6-8 VP end-game dragon can outperform many if-activated dragons
Conditions: dragon size, trait, location, filled columns, etc.

summary.title: Dragon Traits and Synergies
bullets: 4 traits: shy, aggressive, playful, helpful
Some abilities reference traits (tuck per playful dragon, VP per trait match)
Build caves with matching traits for combo potential
End-game abilities may score based on trait distribution

summary.title: Late-Game Coin Efficiency
bullets: Round 4: maximize score per coin
Explore deepest cave first for most ability triggers
Consider exploring Amethyst Abyss twice for hatchling caching
Leftover coins = 1 VP each; convert surplus resources into cached VP

entries.definition: An ability that triggers once, immediately when the dragon or cave card is first placed on your player mat. It does not trigger again. Shown with a specific icon on the card.

An ability on a dragon that triggers each time your adventurer walks onto that dragon during an explore action. If-activated abilities do NOT trigger the turn you play the dragon. Each activation is optional.

An ability that triggers at the end of every round during the upkeep phase. Players activate these in turn order starting with the start player. If it benefits all players, they choose in clockwise order from the dragon's owner.
An ability that provides victory points at the end of the game for meeting certain conditions, such as having a certain number of dragons of a specific size, trait, or in a specific cave.

Place a resource token from your personal supply onto a dragon card. Unless otherwise specified, resources must be cached onto the dragon granting the ability. Cached resources remain until game end and are worth 1 VP each.

Place a face-down dragon card underneath a dragon already on your player mat. Unless otherwise specified, tucked cards come from your hand. Tucked cards are worth 1 VP each at game end but do NOT count as dragons for abilities or objectives.

Place an egg from the supply onto any dragon with an empty nest icon or onto one of the two nest icons on your player mat. Each nest icon holds 1 egg. Some abilities specify laying an egg 'here' (only on that dragon) or in a certain cave.
Discard an egg from any dragon on your player mat or from a nest on your player mat. Some abilities specify paying from a certain cave or location.
Move your guild token 1 space clockwise on the Dragon Guild track and gain the benefit on the landing space. Earned through explore actions, dragon abilities, and cave card abilities.

Some abilities benefit all players. The active player resolves first, then others in clockwise order. Opponents must be given the option to take their benefit even if the active player declines theirs.

The top row of the player mat. One of three caves in your sanctuary. Its built-in explore abilities grant gold, guild advancements, and resources. Starts with 1 excavated space and can hold up to 4 dragons.

The middle row of the player mat. One of three caves in your sanctuary. Its built-in explore abilities grant gold, eggs, and guild advancements. Starts with 1 excavated space and can hold up to 4 dragons.

The bottom row of the player mat. One of three caves in your sanctuary. Its built-in explore abilities grant gold, resources, and eggs. Most hatchlings prefer this cave. Starts with 1 excavated space and can hold up to 4 dragons.
A space on your player mat that has been opened with a cave card and can now hold a dragon. Each cave starts with 1 pre-excavated space. You must excavate from left to right.
The starting position on your player mat where your adventurer returns at the end of every turn. Located to the left of all three caves.

Square cards played onto empty spaces on your player mat through the excavate action. Each cave card opens one space for a dragon and often provides a when-played benefit. Any cave card can go in any cave; colors are cosmetic.

Rectangular cards representing dragons that are played onto excavated spaces through the entice action. Each dragon has a resource cost, preferred caves, an ability, a size, a trait, and nest icons for egg storage.

A special type of dragon that costs an egg plus 1-3 milk to play. Hatchlings have a three-part ability: an if-activated cache/tuck, a recurring bonus per cache/tuck, and a one-time growth bonus on the 3rd cache/tuck. Most prefer the Amethyst Abyss.
One of four dragon sizes. Large dragons tend to have higher VP values and costs. Referenced by some end-of-round objectives and end-game abilities.
One of four dragon sizes. Medium dragons offer a balance of cost and ability. Referenced by some objectives and dragon abilities.
One of four dragon sizes. Small dragons are typically cheaper to play and have useful abilities. Referenced by some objectives and dragon abilities.
The smallest dragon size. All hatchlings share the special three-part ability structure. They require milk and an egg to play. Referenced by some objectives and abilities.
One of four dragon traits. Some abilities reference shy dragons for bonuses, tucking, or end-game scoring. Each dragon has exactly one trait.
One of four dragon traits. Some abilities reference aggressive dragons for bonuses, such as gaining resources for each aggressive dragon in a cave.
One of four dragon traits. Some abilities reference playful dragons, such as tucking a card under each playful dragon in a cave when activated.
One of four dragon traits. Some abilities reference helpful dragons. Dragons with 'all players' abilities are often (but not always) helpful.
One of four resource types. Shown as yellow tokens. Used to pay dragon costs and as a general resource. Gained from cave abilities, Dragon Guild, and various dragon powers.
One of four resource types. Shown as purple tokens. Used to pay dragon costs. Particularly important for hatchlings and Amethyst Abyss dragons.
One of four resource types. Shown as red tokens. Used to pay dragon costs and to cache onto hatchlings. Common cost for large and aggressive dragons.
One of four resource types. Shown as white tokens. Required to play all hatchlings (1-3 milk each). Also used as costs for some adult dragons.

Silver tokens used as the primary action currency. Every action costs at least 1 coin. Players gain 6 coins at the start of each round. Coins are NOT resources and cannot be used for resource exchanges. Remaining coins at game end are worth 1 VP each.

Used both as a cost (excavating columns 3-4, exploring caves 2nd/3rd time, playing hatchlings) and as a scoring element (1 VP each at game end). Stored on nest icons on dragons and player mat. Players gain 1 egg at the start of each round.
A symbol on dragon cards and the player mat where eggs can be stored. Each nest icon holds exactly 1 egg. Dragons may have 0, 1, or 2 nest icons. The player mat has 2 built-in nest icons.
At any time during the game, you may trade 2 resource tokens (gold, crystal, meat, milk in any combination) for 1 resource of a different type. This does not apply to coins or eggs.

At the end of each of your turns, you may have a maximum of 9 resource tokens, 9 cards (dragons + cave cards combined), and 9 coins. Discard excess. If you exceed limits during another player's turn, you do not discard until your next turn ends.

Icons on the left side of a dragon card showing which caves the dragon can live in. You must play a dragon into one of its preferred caves. Some dragons prefer only one cave; others may prefer two or all three.

Your meeple that walks through caves during explore actions. Moves left to right, activating abilities along the way. Returns to Base Camp at the end of every turn when it reaches an unexcavated space.
Your token on the Dragon Guild track. Advances clockwise when you earn guild advancements. Landing on brown spaces triggers marker placement on the Guild tile.
8 markers per player in their color. 4 are used for public objective scoring on the round tracker. 4 are used for the Dragon Guild tile bonuses.
The central board with a circular track of spaces. Players advance guild tokens around the track to gain benefits. Brown spaces trigger marker placement on the Guild tile for VP and bonuses.
One of 4 double-sided tiles placed on the Guild board. Each tile offers different bonuses when players place markers on it. Abilities include free excavation, reduced dragon costs, and end-game VP.
The board showing 3 face-up dragon cards and 3 face-up cave cards available for purchase or abilities. Refreshed at the end of each player's turn (individual cards) and fully at round start.
Tracks the current round (1-4) and holds the 4 objective tiles. Players place markers here each round for objective scoring. Has competitive scoring positions: 1st, 2nd, 3rd, and All Others.

4 double-sided objective tiles placed on the round tracker, one scored per round. Objectives reward things like number of hatchlings, large dragons, dragons in a specific cave, or cached resources. Ties are friendly.

When two or more players tie for a position on a public objective, all tied players gain full VP for that position. The next player takes the next lower position. For example, two players tied for 1st both get 1st-place VP; the next gets 2nd.
The victory point value printed on each dragon card. Only visible (non-tucked) dragons on your player mat count. One of the primary scoring categories.
At game end, gain 1 VP per remaining coin and 1 VP per 4 remaining items (resources, dragon cards, cave cards in hand, in any combination, rounded down).
Do not refill dragon or cave cards into the display until the end of each player's turn. At the start of each new round, discard all 6 display cards and deal 3 fresh dragons and 3 fresh cave cards.

Unless restricted, you may gain a card from either the face-up display or the top of the deck. 'From the display' means face-up only. 'Draw' means from the top of the deck only. 'Reveal' means show the top card to all players.
A benefit printed on certain player mat spaces that triggers when a cave card is placed on top of them during excavation. Typically found on 3rd and 4th column spaces.
The symbol on unexcavated spaces that ends your adventurer's movement during an explore action. When the adventurer reaches a stop sign, the turn ends and the adventurer returns to Base Camp.
